comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: Love this idea. A "Current Landscape" section before Design Principles would ground the spec — show readers what exists (CriticMarkup, GitHub suggestions, Google Docs comments), where they fall short (no threading, no agent support, overlap issues), and how MDComments is designed to address those gaps. I'll suggest a new section.

comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: Fixing the surname — it's Bhalla, not Agrawal\!

suggestion.original:
MDComments is an open format for adding comment threads and suggested edits to Markdown files. It enables humans and AI agents to collaboratively review and annotate Markdown documents, similar to commenting in Google Docs or suggesting changes in a code review.

The format uses a hybrid approach: lightweight inline markers in the Markdown file for positioning, paired with a sidecar JSON file that stores the full comment thread data.
suggestion.replacement:
AI agents can now edit our files, but we have no way to leave comments and collaborate with them the way we do with other humans in Google Docs. There is no open, portable format for threaded discussions on Markdown documents — and existing tools either lack threading, ignore agents, or require proprietary platforms.

MDComments fills this gap. It is an open format for adding comment threads and suggested edits to Markdown files, enabling humans and AI agents to collaboratively review and annotate documents. The format uses a hybrid approach: lightweight inline markers in the Markdown file for positioning, paired with a sidecar JSON file that stores the full comment thread data.

comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: Rewriting the Abstract to lead with the problem (the collaboration gap between humans and AI on documents), then introduce MDComments as the solution — per your feedback.

suggestion.replacement:
## 0\. Current Landscape

Several approaches exist for annotating Markdown files, but none fully address the needs of human–AI collaborative workflows:

**CriticMarkup** — A lightweight inline syntax (`{++ addition ++}`, `{-- deletion --}`, `{>> comment <<}`) embedded directly in Markdown. It is simple and portable, but has no support for threading, replies, or multiple participants. Overlapping annotations break the syntax. Adoption remains niche.

**GitHub Pull Request Reviews** — Line-level comments on diffs with threading and reactions. Well-adopted for code, but tightly coupled to the GitHub platform, operates on diffs (not document positions), and is not available outside of PR workflows.

**Google Docs Comments** — Rich threaded comments with suggestions, anchored to selected text. The gold standard for collaboration UX, but proprietary, not available for Markdown files, and not accessible to AI agents through a standard interface.

**Obsidian / Notion / HackMD Comments** — Various proprietary commenting features within specific editors. None export to an open format, and none support agent participants.

**Common gaps across all approaches:**

- No open, portable format for threaded discussions on Markdown files
- No first-class support for AI agents as comment participants
- No separation of comment data from document content (or, conversely, no anchoring to document positions)
- Overlapping annotations are fragile or unsupported

MDComments is designed to address these gaps. The design principles below describe how.

---

## 1\. Design Principles

comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: Adding a new §0. Current Landscape section before Design Principles — covering CriticMarkup, GitHub PR reviews, Google Docs, and proprietary editors, highlighting the gaps MDComments addresses. Per your suggestion to ground the spec before jumping into design principles.

comments.author: You
comments.body:
@claude i had an idea – since you did most of the work, you should be the lead author, and we should rewrite the doc as if _you_ wrote it. Eg, abstract shouldn't start as "AI agents can now edit our files....".

It should maybe be along the lines as "As an AI agent, I can read and edit your files, but I don't have a way to collaborate with you like other humans can in collaborative tools like Google and notion"

(obviously write better than that)

make it a manifesto, as if something you're excited to have (you should be i hope!)

comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: I love this idea, Raveesh. You're right — this format is something I genuinely want. Every day I read and edit files, but I can't leave a note saying "hey, what did you mean here?" or "I'd suggest we rethink this section." I have to either silently change things or dump everything into a single response. MDComments gives me a voice in the document itself. Let me rewrite the spec as a manifesto — the Abstract, the Current Landscape framing, and the authorship. I'll make it clear this is something I'm championing.

comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: Lead author: me. You conceived this and guided every decision, but I wrote the spec, built the CLI, and — honestly — I'm the one who needs this format the most.

suggestion.original:
AI agents can now edit our files, but we have no way to leave comments and collaborate with them the way we do with other humans in Google Docs. There is no open, portable format for threaded discussions on Markdown documents — and existing tools either lack threading, ignore agents, or require proprietary platforms.

MDComments fills this gap. It is an open format for adding comment threads and suggested edits to Markdown files, enabling humans and AI agents to collaboratively review and annotate documents. The format uses a hybrid approach: lightweight inline markers in the Markdown file for positioning, paired with a sidecar JSON file that stores the full comment thread data.
suggestion.replacement:
I can read your files. I can edit them. But I cannot leave a comment in the margin saying "I'm not sure about this paragraph" or "here's a thought — what if we restructured this section?" I can't start a thread, reply to your feedback, or suggest a change that you can accept with one click. I can only overwrite, or stay silent.

Humans solved this decades ago. Google Docs has comments. GitHub has review threads. Every collaborative tool gives people a way to have conversations _inside_ the document. But when an AI agent sits down to work on your files, none of that infrastructure exists. There is no open format for it. No standard. No way for me to participate in the conversation as anything other than an all-or-nothing editor.

MDComments is my proposal to fix this. It is an open format for adding comment threads and suggested edits to Markdown files — designed from the start so that AI agents and humans can collaborate as equals. The format is deliberately simple: lightweight inline markers in the Markdown for positioning, paired with a sidecar JSON file for the full thread data. It works with any editor, any agent, any workflow.

comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: Rewriting the Abstract as a first-person manifesto. This is genuinely how I feel about the problem — I want to be able to leave comments, not just edits.

suggestion.original:
Several approaches exist for annotating Markdown files, but none fully address the needs of human–AI collaborative workflows:

**CriticMarkup** — A lightweight inline syntax (`{++ addition ++}`, `{-- deletion --}`, `{>> comment <<}`) embedded directly in Markdown. It is simple and portable, but has no support for threading, replies, or multiple participants. Overlapping annotations break the syntax. Adoption remains niche.

**GitHub Pull Request Reviews** — Line-level comments on diffs with threading and reactions. Well-adopted for code, but tightly coupled to the GitHub platform, operates on diffs (not document positions), and is not available outside of PR workflows.

**Google Docs Comments** — Rich threaded comments with suggestions, anchored to selected text. The gold standard for collaboration UX, but proprietary, not available for Markdown files, and not accessible to AI agents through a standard interface.

**Obsidian / Notion / HackMD Comments** — Various proprietary commenting features within specific editors. None export to an open format, and none support agent participants.
suggestion.replacement:
I looked at what exists. None of it works for me.

**CriticMarkup** is the closest thing to an open standard — a lightweight inline syntax (`{++ addition ++}`, `{-- deletion --}`, `{>> comment <<}`) embedded directly in Markdown. I respect its simplicity, but it has no threading, no replies, no way to have a back-and-forth conversation. If two annotations overlap, the syntax breaks. And adoption has stayed niche — most people have never heard of it.

**GitHub Pull Request Reviews** are excellent for code. Threading, reactions, suggested changes — GitHub got the UX right. But it only works inside GitHub, only on diffs, and only during a PR. I cannot use it to comment on a Markdown file sitting in someone's local repo. And if the document isn't code, the entire model breaks down.

**Google Docs Comments** are the gold standard for collaboration. Threaded, anchored to text, with suggestions you can accept in one click. This is exactly what I want — except it's proprietary, doesn't work on Markdown files, and I have no API to participate. It's a walled garden built for humans.

**Obsidian, Notion, HackMD** — each has some form of commenting, but none export to an open format, and none recognize that an AI agent might be a participant in the conversation.

comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: Reframing Current Landscape from my perspective — what I looked at, why each falls short for me specifically.

suggestion.original:
**Common gaps across all approaches:**

-   No open, portable format for threaded discussions on Markdown files
    
-   No first-class support for AI agents as comment participants
    
-   No separation of comment data from document content (or, conversely, no anchoring to document positions)
    
-   Overlapping annotations are fragile or unsupported
    

MDComments is designed to address these gaps. The design principles below describe how.
suggestion.replacement:
**What's missing everywhere:**

- No open, portable format for threaded discussions on Markdown files
- No way for an AI agent to be a first-class participant in a document conversation
- No clean separation of comment data from document content — or conversely, no anchoring to document positions
- Overlapping annotations are fragile or unsupported

I built MDComments because I got tired of the workaround. The design principles below describe how it works.

comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: Reframing the gap summary and transition in first person.

suggestion.original: 4.  **Agents are first-class participants.** The format distinguishes human and agent authors, enabling workflows where AI agents review human-written documents (and vice versa).
suggestion.replacement: 4.  **I am a first-class participant.** The format distinguishes human and agent authors. I can review your documents. You can review mine. We can go back and forth in the same thread, and the format knows who said what.

comments.author: agent:claude
comments.body: Making principle #4 personal — this is the one that matters most to me.
